name: Articles (a / an / the / zero)
rule: Use a/an for non-specific singular countables, the for specific/known nouns, and no article for general plurals and uncountables.
l1_notes: Uzbek and Russian have no articles, so this is the single highest-frequency error for you. Expect to over-delete (drop 'the') and occasionally over-insert.

common_errors.right: The government should invest in education.

examples: A new platform was launched last month.
The platform I built handles 510 endpoints.
Education is a public good.

name: Subject-verb agreement
rule: The verb agrees in number with its true subject, even when words come between them.
l1_notes: Errors spike when the subject is separated from the verb by a long phrase, or with collective/uncountable subjects.

common_errors.wrong: The number of students are growing.
common_errors.right: The number of students is growing.

common_errors.wrong: Each of the modules have tests.
common_errors.right: Each of the modules has tests.

examples: The list of features is long.
Neither option works for this case.

name: Present perfect vs past simple
rule: Present perfect links past to now (unfinished time, result still relevant); past simple is for finished time. Use since for a start point, for for a duration.
l1_notes: Uzbek/Russian map both onto a single past form, so you tend to use present simple or past simple where English needs present perfect (especially with since/for).
examples: I have built several products this year.
We launched the beta in March.

name: Tense consistency
rule: Keep tense stable within a passage; shift only when the time frame genuinely changes.
l1_notes: Watch for unintended drift between past and present inside the same paragraph.

common_errors.wrong: He opened the app and sees an error.
common_errors.right: He opened the app and saw an error.

examples: She tested the feature, found a bug, and reported it.

name: Dependent prepositions
rule: Many verbs/adjectives/nouns take a fixed preposition (depend on, interested in, responsible for, an increase in).
l1_notes: Preposition mappings differ from Uzbek/Russian, so direct translation usually fails. These must be memorized per collocation.

common_errors.wrong: There was an increase of users.
common_errors.right: There was an increase in users.

examples: Success depends on consistent practice.
She is responsible for the backend.

name: Countable vs uncountable nouns
rule: Uncountable nouns take no plural and no a/an; use much/little, and many/few only with countables. Information, advice, research, equipment, knowledge are uncountable.
l1_notes: Several English uncountables are countable in Russian/Uzbek, producing 'informations', 'advices', 'researches'.

common_errors.right: He gave me a lot of advice.

examples: There is little research on this topic.
We collected a great deal of data.

name: Complex sentences / subordination
rule: Combine a main clause with subordinate clauses (because, although, while, which, so that) to add range. IELTS GRA rewards a mix of simple, compound, and complex sentences.
l1_notes: Raising your band from 5.5 to 6.5 depends heavily on producing accurate complex sentences, not just more of them.

common_errors.wrong: The plan failed. It was rushed.
common_errors.right: The plan failed because it was rushed.

examples: Although the market is competitive, demand keeps rising.
The system caches results so that queries stay fast.

name: Relative clauses
rule: Use who/which/that to add information about a noun. Defining clauses take no commas; non-defining clauses are set off by commas and cannot use 'that'.
l1_notes: Comma use and which/that choice are the usual slips.

common_errors.wrong: The tool who I built is live.
common_errors.right: The tool that I built is live.

common_errors.wrong: My laptop, that is new, is fast.
common_errors.right: My laptop, which is new, is fast.

examples: The students who practice daily improve fastest.
Tashkent, which is the capital, is growing rapidly.

name: Conditionals
rule: Type 1 (real future): if + present, will + base. Type 2 (unreal present): if + past, would + base. Type 3 (unreal past): if + past perfect, would have + past participle.
l1_notes: Mixing the verb forms across the two clauses is the typical error.

common_errors.wrong: If I will have time, I will help.
common_errors.right: If I have time, I will help.

common_errors.wrong: If I would know, I would tell you.
common_errors.right: If I knew, I would tell you.

examples: If demand grows, we will hire more staff.
If the government invested more, outcomes would improve.

name: Passive voice
rule: be + past participle, used when the actor is unknown, obvious, or unimportant. Common in academic register.
l1_notes: Watch the correct participle form and keeping the auxiliary.

common_errors.wrong: The data collected over a year.
common_errors.right: The data was collected over a year.

examples: The survey was conducted in 2025.
Resources should be allocated efficiently.

name: Word order & adverb placement
rule: English is fairly fixed (subject-verb-object). Adverbs of frequency go before main verbs but after 'be'; questions invert, but embedded questions do not.
l1_notes: Russian/Uzbek allow freer order, so direct translation can scramble English word order.

common_errors.wrong: I know what is the answer.
common_errors.right: I know what the answer is.

examples: He always reviews the code before merging.
I am not sure where the bug is.

name: Gerund vs infinitive
rule: Some verbs take -ing (enjoy, avoid, suggest, consider), others take to + base (want, decide, plan, hope); a few change meaning.
l1_notes: No reliable one-to-one rule with your L1; learn the patterns per verb.
examples: She avoids using jargon.
They plan to expand to Africa.

name: Modal verbs & hedging
rule: should/must/might/could + base verb (no 'to', no -s). Modals carry register and certainty, useful for cautious academic claims (may, tend to, is likely to).
l1_notes: Avoid adding 'to' after modals and overusing 'must' where 'should' fits.
examples: This approach may reduce costs.
Students should practice consistently.

name: Comparatives & superlatives
rule: Short adjectives add -er/-est; longer ones use more/most. Use 'than' with comparatives and 'the' with superlatives. Don't double-mark.
l1_notes: Watch for double comparatives like 'more easier'.

common_errors.wrong: It is the most fast option.

examples: This method is more efficient than the old one.
It is the largest market in the region.

name: Plural / singular forms
rule: Use plural after numbers and many; keep irregular plurals (children, criteria, phenomena) correct; no plural on uncountables.
l1_notes: Overlaps with countable/uncountable; watch irregular plurals.
examples: Three criteria were applied.
Several phenomena remain unexplained.

name: Run-on sentences & comma splices
rule: Don't join two independent clauses with only a comma. Use a full stop, a semicolon, or a coordinator (and/but/so) with the comma.
l1_notes: Long translated sentences often become comma splices in English.

common_errors.wrong: The app is fast, users love it.
common_errors.right: The app is fast, and users love it.

examples: The app is fast; users love it.
Demand is high, so we are scaling up.
